macro_segments.title: Aggadic teachings on gender and procreation: Male and female roles in the world

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yitzchak's teaching: When a male comes to the world, peace comes to the world
The male comes with his loaf of bread; the female comes empty-handed
Discussion of essentialist versus societal framework for gender roles
Why male brings shalom (peace/security/protection) to the world

macro_segments.title: Why a woman bringing a korban after childbirth: The oath during labor

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai's students ask why a woman brings a korban
Answer: She swears not to have relations due to childbirth pain
Rav Yosef's objection: She is a mezid, not a shogeg; how can a chatas apply?

macro_segments.title: The different tumah periods for male and female newborns: One week versus two weeks

micro_segments.title: Why male child has one week tumah, female child has two weeks
Male brings joy (shemcha), so mother happy after one week; female brings sadness, so two weeks
Contemporary poskim ruling on making brachot for daughters

macro_segments.title: Why the niddah period is seven days: Refreshing the marriage through separation

micro_segments.title: Why is niddah seven days when other natural tumaos are one day?
Answer: Husband becomes accustomed; separation makes her dear like a wedding night
Modern parallels: Frequency decline in long-term marriages and the role of novelty
Maimonides' analogy: Beis HaMikdash and appreciation through restricted access

macro_segments.title: Aggadic teachings on gender relations: Why men pursue women and physical positions

micro_segments.title: Why man pursues woman: She is the lost object he seeks
Why man looks downward, woman looks upward in marital relations
Why man is easily appeased but not woman: Pliability of earth versus bone
Why woman has beautiful voice: Bone is more resonant than earth

macro_segments.title: The status of Samaritan women as always niddah: Practical application and rationale

micro_segments.title: Samaritan women and men treated as always impure from niddah perspective
Why Samaritan women are niddah: They do not distinguish blood colors
Stringency becomes leniency: Early counting leads to going to mikvah while still niddah
Status of doubt: No karet for entering Beis HaMikdash; no burning of terumah

macro_segments.title: Gemara's investigation: When do we assume infants have natural body discharges?

micro_segments.title: Heichi dami: Do Samaritan infants actually bleed, or do they not?
Rava: We don't know; there is a tiny percentage where infants bleed
Rabbi Meir's principle: Concerned about minority when not rare; not when completely rare
Story of Ein Bul and Beit She'arim: Girl taken to mikvah before mother

macro_segments.title: Why is concern for Samaritan infants but not our own infants? The role of tradition (drasha)

micro_segments.title: We derive from Tanakh that even underage girls can become niddah
We separate them appropriately; Samaritans don't know this drasha
Therefore we have gezeirah only for Samaritans who ignore the halacha

macro_segments.title: Deriving underage female tumah from Tanakh: The extra vav by niddah

micro_segments.title: Baraita: Woman, from the word isha, but an infant girl can become niddah
Extra vav teaches that underage girls become niddah, going back to one day old
Why repeat the derasha about extra vav for zavah if we can infer from niddah?
Extra vav by zavah excludes the male: Red discharge only applies to women

macro_segments.title: Deriving underage male tumah from Tanakh: The double ish (ish ish) for zav

micro_segments.title: Why ish ish teaches infants can become zav; Rav Sheshet and Rabbi Yishmael
Rabbi Yishmael: No need for ish ish; Torah says zachar u'nekevah (male and female)
Torah speaks in human language; not derasha needed

macro_segments.title: Why we need both male and female drashos: Different strictness levels of tumah

micro_segments.title: Stricter male zav: Multiple sightings suffice, even same day; so presumed for infants
Female zavah: Three days required; stricter still if by ones (trauma); so need separate drasha
Cannot infer one gender from other; need both explicit teachings

macro_segments.title: Mishnah: Tumah status and legal capacity of newborns from day one

micro_segments.title: One-day-old girl can become niddah; ten-day-old girl can become zavah
One-day-old boy can become zav and is affected by nega'im and tumah meit
One-day-old child: exempt from yibum, permits mother to eat terumah, disqualifies mother
One-day-old child: can inherit, pass inheritance, liable for murder

topical_tags.term: Zachar (male) as carrier of production and shalom
Nekeivah (female) as dependent, empty-handed
Essentialist vs. societal framework for gender differences
Korban after childbirth; labor oath interpretation
Tumah and taharah: טומאה וטהרה distinction between cleansing and sin
Rav Yosef's objection to korban: mezid vs. shogeg
One week tumah male, two weeks tumah female (child)
Simcha (joy) and atzavim (sadness) determining tumah duration
Brit milah on day eight; husband and wife together at simcha
Niddah seven-day separation to refresh marital relationship
Familiarity breeds contempt/boredom in long-term marriage
Rambam Guide to Perplexed: Beis HaMikdash and tamah/taharah cycles
Aveidah (lost object): Man pursues woman as recovering what was lost
Etzem mi'atzamai (bone of my bones): Man finds completed self in woman
Downward/upward facing in marital relations; earth vs. bone
Piyus (appeasement): Man more easily appeased than woman
Voice beauty: Woman's pleasing voice vs. earth's lack of melody
Benot Kutim (Samaritan women) always considered niddah
Kutim (Samaritans) as ambiguous status: neither fully Jewish nor non-Jewish
Gezeirah (rabbinic decree) against Samaritans for niddah purposes
Kutim do not distinguish blood colors; considered niddah always
Chumra ati lidei kula (stringency leading to leniency): Early counting error
Safek (doubt) status: No karet for entering Beis HaMikdash with Samaritan
Terumah and tumah: Concern for unwitting violation through Samaritans
Sicha k'shtiya (rubbing oil like drinking): Applies to tumah of terumah
Drasha (hermeneutical derivation) for underage niddah from Torah
Extra vav (ו) in Torah to include underage cases
Minut (minority cases) and shekhiach (common): Rabbi Meir's concern threshold
Infant girl niddah from day one of life
Infant girl zavah from day ten (after seven days of niddah plus three bleeding days)
Ish ish (double masculine) for including underage boys as zav
Rabbi Yishmael's alternative: zachar u'nekevah suffices without ish ish
Dibbra Torah k'lishon bnei adam (Torah speaks in human language)
Three years and one day: Minimum age for act of sex (bi'ah) with girl
Nine years and one day: Minimum age for boy for act of sex (bi'ah)
Male zav: Multiple sightings (ire'iyot) suffice, same day possible
Female zavah: Three separate days required; stricter if by ones (trauma)
No cross-gender inference: Need separate drashos for male and female
Niddah one day old; becomes niddah (טמאה temeiah) with any blood
Zavah ten days old; three days of blood on separate days in days 8-11
Zav one day old; three separate sightings of discharge
Nega'im (skin lesions) and tumah meit (corpse impurity) from day one
Yibum (levirate marriage): One-day-old exempts mother; can necessitate yibum
Terumah eligibility: One-day-old enables/disqualifies mother's eating
Inheritance and heirship: One-day-old can inherit and pass inheritance
Murder liability: One-day-old technically liable for killing (not executed)
